title: Find first and last index of target
description: In a sorted array find first and last index of target element

examples.explanation: Initialize first = -1, last = -1. i=0: arr[0]=1 ≠ 2. i=1: arr[1]=2 == 2, first=-1 so set first=1, last=1. i=2: arr[2]=2 == 2, first≠-1 so update last=2. i=3: arr[3]=2 == 2, update last=3. i=4: arr[4]=3 ≠ 2. i=5: arr[5]=4 ≠ 2. i=6: arr[6]=5 ≠ 2. Loop ends. Return [1, 3].

Initialize first = -1, last = -1. i=0: arr[0]=5 ≠ 8. i=1: arr[1]=7 ≠ 8. i=2: arr[2]=7 ≠ 8. i=3: arr[3]=8 == 8, first=-1 so set first=3, last=3. i=4: arr[4]=8 == 8, update last=4. i=5: arr[5]=10 ≠ 8. Loop ends. Return [3, 4].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array from start to end, tracking the first and last occurrence of the target. Use this for small arrays or when simplicity is prioritized over efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Search for First and Last Index
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding first and last occurrence of target in sorted array using linear scan.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the problem. The intuition is straightforward: since the array is sorted, all occurrences of the target will be contiguous. We can simply scan through the entire array once, tracking when we first encounter the target and when we last see it. The algorithm initializes two variables (first and last) to -1 (indicating not found). Then it iterates through each element of the array. When it finds an element equal to the target, if first is still -1, this is the first occurrence, so we set both first and last to the current index. For subsequent occurrences, we only update last to the current index. After the loop, we return [first, last]. Time complexity is O(n) since we potentially examine every element. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is simple and works for any array size, though it's inefficient for large arrays compared to binary search. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty arrays (should return [-1, -1]), not initializing first/last properly, or incorrectly updating only one index when finding the first occurrence. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the linear scan subroutine as its core mechanism.

examples.trace: Initialize first = -1, last = -1. i=0: arr[0]=1 ≠ 2. i=1: arr[1]=2 == 2, first=-1 so set first=1, last=1. i=2: arr[2]=2 == 2, first≠-1 so update last=2. i=3: arr[3]=2 == 2, update last=3. i=4: arr[4]=3 ≠ 2. i=5: arr[5]=4 ≠ 2. i=6: arr[6]=5 ≠ 2. Loop ends. Return [1, 3].

Initialize first = -1, last = -1. i=0: arr[0]=5 ≠ 8. i=1: arr[1]=7 ≠ 8. i=2: arr[2]=7 ≠ 8. i=3: arr[3]=8 == 8, first=-1 so set first=3, last=3. i=4: arr[4]=8 == 8, update last=4. i=5: arr[5]=10 ≠ 8. Loop ends. Return [3, 4].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Scan to Find Target Range
ladders.desc: Implement a function that scans array once to find first and last index of target.
ladders.explanation: This is the core subroutine of the brute force approach. The intuition is that by scanning the array sequentially, we can identify all occurrences of the target and record the boundaries. The algorithm maintains two pointers/variables: first_occurrence and last_occurrence, both initialized to -1. As we iterate through each index i from 0 to n-1, we check if arr[i] equals the target. If yes and first_occurrence is -1, we've found the first occurrence, so we set both first_occurrence and last_occurrence to i. If yes and first_occurrence is not -1, we've found a subsequent occurrence, so we update last_occurrence to i. After the loop, we return the pair [first_occurrence, last_occurrence]. Time complexity is O(n) for scanning the array. Space complexity is O(1). This subroutine directly implements the search logic without any additional setup. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop bounds, not handling the case where target appears only once (should set both first and last to same index), or incorrectly resetting first_occurrence when finding later occurrences. The connection to L0 is that this subroutine IS the complete solution - L0 just wraps it in proper function structure. The connection to L2 is that this subroutine relies on the core logic of tracking first and last indices during iteration.

examples.trace: first=-1, last=-1. i=0: arr[0]=1 ≠ 2. i=1: arr[1]=1 ≠ 2. i=2: arr[2]=2 == 2, first=-1 so set first=2, last=2. i=3: arr[3]=2 == 2, update last=3. i=4: arr[4]=2 == 2, update last=4. i=5: arr[5]=3 ≠ 2. Return [2, 4].
first=-1, last=-1. i=0: arr[0]=4 ≠ 5. i=1: arr[1]=5 == 5, first=-1 so set first=1, last=1. i=2: arr[2]=6 ≠ 5. Return [1, 1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking First and Last Occurrence During Iteration
ladders.desc: Learn to maintain two variables that track the boundaries of target occurrences while scanning.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central technique of the brute force approach: maintaining two variables (first and last) that get updated as we find the target during iteration. The intuition is that since we're scanning left to right, the first time we encounter the target must be the first occurrence, and the last time we encounter it must be the last occurrence (because all occurrences are contiguous in a sorted array). The algorithm: Initialize first = -1, last = -1. For each index i from 0 to n-1: If arr[i] == target: If first == -1: This is the first occurrence, so set first = i and last = i. Else: This is a subsequent occurrence, so update last = i. After loop, first and last contain the answer. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop. Space complexity is O(1). The key insight is that we only need to update 'last' for subsequent occurrences, not 'first'. This works because the array is sorted, so all target elements are grouped together. Common mistakes include: setting last = i on the first occurrence but forgetting to set first, or vice versa; incorrectly using separate flags; or trying to optimize by breaking early (can't because we need to find the last occurrence). The connection to L1 is that this core logic is exactly what the linear scan subroutine implements. The connection to L3 is that this logic relies on the building block of comparing array elements to the target.

examples.trace: first=-1, last=-1. i=0: arr[0]=3 == 3, first=-1 so set first=0, last=0. i=1: arr[1]=3 == 3, first≠-1 so update last=1. i=2: arr[2]=3 == 3, update last=2. i=3: arr[3]=4 ≠ 3. i=4: arr[4]=5 ≠ 3. Final: first=0, last=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Array Elements to Target Value
ladders.desc: Practice iterating through an array and checking if each element equals a target value.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental to the brute force approach: scanning through an array and comparing each element to a target value. The intuition is simple: to find where a value occurs, we need to examine each position and check if the element there matches what we're looking for. The algorithm: Given an array arr of length n and a target value, iterate through each index i from 0 to n-1. For each i, check if arr[i] == target. If true, we've found an occurrence at index i. We can choose to record this index, count occurrences, or take other action. Time complexity is O(n) since we visit each element once. Space complexity is O(1) if we just check without storing all matches. This building block is essential because the brute force solution extends this by not just checking for equality, but tracking the first and last matching indices. Common mistakes include: incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one errors), confusing array indices with array values, or not handling empty arrays. The connection to L2 is that this comparison operation is what allows us to identify target occurrences during iteration. The connection to L4 is that this building block relies on the basic operation of accessing array elements by index.

examples.trace: i=0: arr[0]=1 ≠ 3. i=1: arr[1]=3 == 3 → found at index 1. i=2: arr[2]=3 == 3 → found at index 2. i=3: arr[3]=5 ≠ 3. Total found at indices [1, 2].
i=0: arr[0]=2 ≠ 5. i=1: arr[1]=4 ≠ 5. i=2: arr[2]=6 ≠ 5. i=3: arr[3]=8 ≠ 5. No indices where arr[i] == 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Learn to retrieve elements from an array using their positional index.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is crucial for any array algorithm: accessing an element at a specific position. In most programming languages, arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The intuition is that an array is a contiguous block of memory where each element has a unique address, and we can compute that address using the base address plus index times element size. To access arr[i], we simply use the index i within bounds 0 ≤ i < n, where n is array length. The algorithm: Given array arr and index i, return arr[i] if i is valid. Time complexity is O(1) for direct access. Space complexity is O(1). This operation is basic but essential because all array algorithms need to examine or modify elements. Common mistakes include: using 1-based indexing (thinking index 1 is first element), accessing out of bounds (i < 0 or i ≥ n), or confusing the index with the value stored at that index. The connection to L3 is that comparing array elements to target requires first accessing those elements. The connection to L5 is that this operation relies on understanding what an array index represents.

examples.trace: Array has indices 0, 1, 2, 3 with values [10, 20, 30, 40]. Index 2 corresponds to the third element (since 0-based). arr[2] = 30.
Array indices: 0→5, 1→1, 2→9, 3→3. Index 0 is the first element. arr[0] = 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how positions are numbered with indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding arrays and indexing. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations. Each element has a unique position called an index. In most programming languages, arrays use zero-based indexing: the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. For an array of length n, valid indices are 0, 1, 2, ..., n-1. The intuition is like house numbering on a street: houses are in order, and we number them starting from 0. This foundation is crucial because all array algorithms rely on accessing elements by their positions. Key points: Arrays have fixed size (usually), elements are stored sequentially, and we can access any element in constant time if we know its index. Time complexity for accessing by index is O(1). Space complexity for storing an array is O(n). Common beginner mistakes: thinking indices start at 1, confusing the index with the value stored at that index, or not understanding that arrays have bounds. The connection to L4 is that accessing array elements requires understanding what indices are and how they work.

examples.trace: This array has 3 elements. Position 1 (first) is index 0, value 'apple'. Position 2 is index 1, value 'banana'. Position 3 is index 2, value 'cherry'. Length n=3, so valid indices are 0, 1, 2.
Array length = 5. Index 0 = first element = 10. Index 1 = second element = 20. Index 2 = third = 30. Index 3 = fourth = 40. Index 4 = fifth = 50. No index 5 exists (out of bounds).

approaches.name: Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Perform two binary searches: one to find the leftmost index and another for the rightmost index of the target. This is optimal for sorted arrays and balances speed with clarity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find First and Last Position of Target in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two binary searches to find leftmost and rightmost indices of target in sorted array.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires finding both the first and last occurrence of a target value in a sorted array. The optimal approach uses two binary searches: one to find the leftmost index and another to find the rightmost index. The intuition is that binary search can be adapted to find boundaries rather than just existence. For the leftmost index, when we find the target, we continue searching left to see if there are earlier occurrences. For the rightmost index, we continue searching right. This gives us O(log n) time complexity while maintaining O(1) space. The algorithm first performs a binary search that biases left: when nums[mid] >= target, we move right = mid, otherwise left = mid + 1. This finds the first position where target could be inserted (leftmost occurrence). Then we perform a second binary search that biases right: when nums[mid] <= target, we move left = mid + 1, otherwise right = mid. This finds the last position where target could be inserted (rightmost occurrence). We then check if the found indices actually contain the target. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, not checking if target exists at found indices, and off-by-one errors in the binary search conditions. This connects to L1 by using the boundary-finding binary search sub-routine twice with different conditions.

Pseudocode:
function searchRange(nums, target):
    if nums is empty: return [-1, -1]
    
    # Find leftmost index
    left = 0
    right = len(nums) - 1
    while left < right:
        mid = left + (right - left) // 2
        if nums[mid] >= target:
            right = mid
        else:
            left = mid + 1
    first = left if nums[left] == target else -1
    
    # Find rightmost index
    left = 0
    right = len(nums) - 1
    while left < right:
        mid = left + (right - left + 1) // 2  # Ceiling division
        if nums[mid] <= target:
            left = mid
        else:
            right = mid - 1
    last = left if nums[left] == target else -1
    
    return [first, last]

Time: O(log n) for two binary searches
Space: O(1) using only pointers

examples.trace:
First binary search (leftmost):
Initial: left=0, right=5
mid=2 (value 7) < 8 → left=3
left=3, right=5
mid=4 (value 8) >= 8 → right=4
left=3, right=4
mid=3 (value 8) >= 8 → right=3
left=3, right=3 → first=3

Second binary search (rightmost):
Initial: left=0, right=5
mid=3 (value 8) <= 8 → left=3
left=3, right=5
mid=4 (value 8) <= 8 → left=4
left=4, right=5
mid=5 (value 10) > 8 → right=4
left=4, right=4 → last=4

Result: [3, 4]

First binary search (leftmost):
Initial: left=0, right=4
mid=2 (value 3) < 6 → left=3
left=3, right=4
mid=3 (value 4) < 6 → left=4
left=4, right=4 → first=4 but nums[4]=5≠6 → first=-1

Since first=-1, we know target doesn't exist, return [-1, -1]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search for Leftmost/Rightmost Boundary
ladders.desc: Adapt binary search to find insertion position of target (leftmost or rightmost).

examples.trace:
Leftmost search:
Initial: left=0, right=4
mid=2 (value 2) >= 2 → right=2
left=0, right=2
mid=1 (value 2) >= 2 → right=1
left=0, right=1
mid=0 (value 1) < 2 → left=1
left=1, right=1 → return 1

Rightmost search:
Initial: left=0, right=4
mid=2 (value 2) <= 2 → left=2
left=2, right=4
mid=3 (value 3) > 2 → right=2
left=2, right=2 → return 2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Modified Binary Search Comparison Rules
ladders.desc: Understanding how changing comparison operators affects binary search behavior.
ladders.explanation:
The core insight is that binary search can be adapted by modifying the comparison condition to find boundaries rather than exact matches. In standard binary search, we check if nums[mid] == target and return immediately. For boundary searches, we use >= or <= comparisons to continue searching in one direction. When searching for leftmost position (first occurrence), we use nums[mid] >= target. This means: if the middle element is greater than or equal to target, the boundary might be at mid or to the left, so we move right = mid. If it's less than target, the boundary must be to the right, so left = mid + 1. For rightmost position (last occurrence), we use nums[mid] <= target. This means: if middle element is less than or equal to target, the boundary might be at mid or to the right, so left = mid. If it's greater, the boundary must be to the left, so right = mid - 1. The intuition is that we're not looking for equality but for the transition point. This logic enables finding insertion positions which correspond to first/last occurrences when the target exists. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operators, not understanding why we use >= for leftmost and <= for rightmost, and confusion about when to use floor vs ceiling division. This feeds into L1 by providing the logical foundation for the boundary search sub-routine.

Pseudocode pattern:
# Leftmost pattern
while left < right:
    mid = left + (right - left) // 2
    if condition_met(nums[mid], target):  # e.g., nums[mid] >= target
        right = mid
    else:
        left = mid + 1

# Rightmost pattern
while left < right:
    mid = left + (right - left + 1) // 2
    if condition_met(nums[mid], target):  # e.g., nums[mid] <= target
        left = mid
    else:
        right = mid - 1

Time: O(log n)
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Using nums[mid] >= target:
left=0, right=3
mid=1 (value 2) >= 2 → right=1
left=0, right=1
mid=0 (value 1) < 2 → left=1
left=1, right=1 → position=1
This finds first index where element >= 2

Using nums[mid] <= target:
left=0, right=3
mid=2 (value 2) <= 2 → left=2
left=2, right=3
mid=3 (value 3) > 2 → right=2
left=2, right=2 → position=2
This finds last index where element <= 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Standard Binary Search Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement classic binary search to find if target exists in sorted array.

examples.trace: left=0, right=4, mid=2 → nums[2]=5 == target → return 2

left=0, right=4, mid=2 → nums[2]=5 > 4 → right=1
left=0, right=1, mid=0 → nums[0]=1 < 4 → left=1
left=1, right=1, mid=1 → nums[1]=3 < 4 → left=2
left=2 > right=1 → return -1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Access array elements and compare values using indices.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation involves accessing elements in an array using their indices and comparing values. Arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. To access an element, we use array[index]. For example, if nums = [10, 20, 30], then nums[0] = 10, nums[1] = 20, nums[2] = 30. We can compare these values using standard comparison operators: == (equal), != (not equal), < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less than or equal), >= (greater than or equal). These comparisons return boolean values (true/false). In binary search, we constantly access the middle element using mid index and compare it with the target value to decide which half to search next. It's crucial to ensure indices are within valid range (0 to length-1) to avoid index out of bounds errors. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (thinking first element is at index 1), accessing indices beyond array bounds, and confusing comparison operators. This operation is the building block for L3 because binary search relies on repeatedly accessing and comparing middle elements.

Pseudocode examples:
# Access element at index i
value = nums[i]

# Compare with target
if value == target:
    # found
elif value < target:
    # target is greater
else:
    # target is less

Time: O(1) per access/comparison
Space: O(1)

examples.trace:
Access nums[2]: array index 2 corresponds to third element → value = 8
Compare 8 == 8 → true
So target found at index 2

Access nums[1]: array index 1 corresponds to second element → value = 7
Compare 7 with target 5: 7 > 5 → true
So value at index 1 is greater than target

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sorted Arrays and Ordering
ladders.desc: Understand what it means for an array to be sorted and how elements are ordered.
ladders.explanation:
A sorted array is an array where elements are arranged in a specific order, typically non-decreasing (ascending) order. In non-decreasing order, each element is less than or equal to the next element: array[i] <= array[i+1] for all valid indices. For example, [1,3,3,5,7] is sorted in non-decreasing order. The opposite is non-increasing (descending) order. Sorting enables efficient algorithms like binary search because we can make assumptions about where values might be located. If we know the array is sorted and we're looking for a target value, we can compare with the middle element: if target is less than the middle element, it must be in the left half (if it exists); if greater, it must be in the right half. This property allows us to eliminate half of the remaining elements with each comparison. Understanding sorted order is foundational because binary search only works on sorted arrays. Common misconceptions include thinking binary search works on unsorted arrays, confusing non-decreasing with strictly increasing (which doesn't allow duplicates), and not recognizing that sorting is a prerequisite. This concept feeds into L4 because array indexing and comparison rely on the sorted property to make meaningful decisions.

Examples:
- Sorted (non-decreasing): [1,2,2,3,4] → each element <= next
- Not sorted: [3,1,4,2] → 3 > 1 violates order
- Sorted (non-increasing): [5,4,3,2,1] → each element >= next

Key property: For any indices i < j in a non-decreasing sorted array, array[i] <= array[j]

examples.trace:
Check each consecutive pair:
2 <= 4 ✓
4 <= 6 ✓
6 <= 8 ✓
8 <= 10 ✓
All comparisons true, so array is sorted

Check consecutive pairs:
5 <= 3 ✗ (5 > 3)
Already found violation at first pair, so array is not sorted

approaches.name: Modified Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Use a single binary search to find the target, then expand outward linearly to find the boundaries. This is a hybrid approach that can be efficient if duplicates are few.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n + k) where k is the number of duplicates

ladders.title: Full Problem — Modified Binary Search with Linear Expansion
ladders.desc: Complete solution using binary search to find target, then linear expansion to find boundaries.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the 'find first and last index of target' problem in a sorted array using a hybrid method. The intuition is that binary search gives us O(log n) time to locate any occurrence of the target, and since the array is sorted, all duplicates of the target will be contiguous. Once we find one occurrence, we can expand linearly in both directions to find the boundaries. This works well when duplicates are few (small k), making the overall time O(log n + k). The algorithm first performs a standard binary search to find any index where the target exists. If found, it expands leftwards by decrementing the index until the element is no longer the target, then expands rightwards similarly. The leftmost and rightmost indices are tracked. If the target is not found, return [-1, -1]. The space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is efficient when k is small compared to n, but degrades to O(n) if many duplicates exist. Connection to the problem: This directly implements the described hybrid approach. Common mistakes include not handling the case when the target is not found, incorrect boundary conditions in binary search, or off-by-one errors in linear expansion. Real code pattern: Use while loops for binary search and expansion, with careful index updates.

examples.trace: Step 1: Binary search for target 2. left=0, right=6, mid=3 (arr[3]=2, found). Step 2: Expand left from index 3: check index 2 (arr[2]=2), index 1 (arr[1]=2), index 0 (arr[0]=1, stop). Leftmost=1. Step 3: Expand right from index 3: check index 4 (arr[4]=3, stop). Rightmost=3. Step 4: Return [1, 3].

Step 1: Binary search for target 8. left=0, right=5, mid=2 (arr[2]=7 < 8, update left=3). left=3, right=5, mid=4 (arr[4]=8, found). Step 2: Expand left from index 4: check index 3 (arr[3]=8), index 2 (arr[2]=7, stop). Leftmost=3. Step 3: Expand right from index 4: check index 5 (arr[5]=10, stop). Rightmost=4. Step 4: Return [3, 4].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search to Find Target Occurrence
ladders.desc: Implement binary search to locate any index of the target in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the hybrid approach, responsible for finding an initial occurrence of the target using binary search in O(log n) time. The intuition is that in a sorted array, binary search efficiently halves the search space by comparing the middle element to the target. If the middle element equals the target, we've found an occurrence and can stop. If it's less, search the right half; if greater, search the left half. This works because the sorted property ensures that all elements to the left are smaller and to the right are larger. The algorithm initializes left and right pointers, then loops while left <= right, calculating mid, comparing arr[mid] to target, and updating pointers accordingly. If found, return the mid index; otherwise, return -1. Time complexity is O(log n), space is O(1). Connection to L0: This finds the starting point for linear expansion. Common mistakes include infinite loops from incorrect pointer updates (e.g., using left = mid instead of mid + 1), off-by-one errors in mid calculation, or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: Use while left <= right, mid = (left + right) // 2, and conditionals to update pointers.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2, arr[2]=3 equals target, return 2.

Step 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2, arr[2]=3 < 6, update left=3. Step 2: left=3, right=4, mid=3, arr[3]=4 < 6, update left=4. Step 3: left=4, right=4, mid=4, arr[4]=5 < 6, update left=5. Step 4: left=5 > right=4, loop ends, return -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Linear Expansion from a Known Index
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array and an index where target exists, expand left and right to find boundaries.
ladders.explanation: This core logic handles the linear expansion part of the hybrid approach. Once binary search finds an index where the target exists, we exploit the sorted property: all duplicates of the target are contiguous. Therefore, we can expand leftwards by decrementing the index until we encounter an element not equal to the target, and similarly expand rightwards. The intuition is that since the array is sorted, moving left will only see the same target or smaller elements, and moving right will only see the same target or larger elements. The algorithm starts from the given index, initializes leftmost and rightmost to that index, then uses while loops to decrement leftmost while arr[leftmost-1] equals target, and increment rightmost while arr[rightmost+1] equals target, ensuring indices stay within bounds. This takes O(k) time where k is the number of duplicates. Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This uses the index from binary search as input. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors by not checking array limits, or incorrect stopping conditions. Real code pattern: Use while loops with boundary checks and comparisons.

examples.trace: Step 1: leftmost=2, rightmost=2. Step 2: Expand left: check index 1 (arr[1]=2 equals target), set leftmost=1; check index 0 (arr[0]=1 not equal, stop). Step 3: Expand right: check index 3 (arr[3]=2 equals target), set rightmost=3; check index 4 (arr[4]=3 not equal, stop). Step 4: Return [1, 3].

Step 1: leftmost=4, rightmost=4. Step 2: Expand left: check index 3 (arr[3]=8 equals target), set leftmost=3; check index 2 (arr[2]=7 not equal, stop). Step 3: Expand right: check index 5 (arr[5]=10 not equal, stop). Step 4: Return [3, 4].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating with While Loops and Boundary Checks
ladders.desc: Use while loops to traverse an array with careful index boundary management.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the technique of using while loops to iterate through an array while checking conditions and ensuring indices remain within valid bounds. The intuition is that while loops allow controlled traversal based on a condition, such as continuing while an index is within the array and a property holds (e.g., element equals target). This is essential for linear expansion in the hybrid approach. The algorithm involves initializing an index, then using a while loop that checks if the index is within [0, n-1] and if the array element meets a condition, updating the index accordingly. For example, to expand left, decrement the index while it's greater than 0 and the previous element equals target. Time complexity depends on the number of iterations, space is O(1). Connection to L2: This implements the expansion loops. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in boundary checks (e.g., using index >= 0 instead of index > 0 for left expansion), or infinite loops from incorrect updates. Real code pattern: Use while index > 0 and arr[index-1] == target: index -= 1 for left expansion, and similarly for right.

examples.trace: Step 1: index = 2. Step 2: While index > 0 and arr[index-1] == 2: index=1 (arr[1]=2), condition true; index=0 (arr[0]=1 not 2, stop). Step 3: Return index=1.
Step 1: index = 4. Step 2: While index < 5 and arr[index+1] == 8: index=5 (arr[5]=10 not 8, stop). Step 3: Return index=4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements and Updating Indices
ladders.desc: Compare elements in an array and adjust index variables based on conditions.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves the fundamental actions of accessing an array element at a given index, comparing it to a value (like the target), and then updating an index variable accordingly. The intuition is that arrays allow O(1) access to elements using indices, and comparisons drive decisions in algorithms like binary search or linear expansion. For example, in binary search, we compare arr[mid] to target to decide whether to go left or right, updating left or right indices. In linear expansion, we compare arr[index-1] to target to decide whether to decrement the index. The algorithm typically uses if-else statements: if arr[i] == target, do something; if arr[i] < target, update index one way; if greater, update another way. Time complexity per operation is O(1), space is O(1). Connection to L3: This is used within the while loops for condition checks. Common mistakes include using incorrect indices (e.g., off-by-one), or not handling edge cases like empty arrays. Real code pattern: Use if arr[mid] == target: return mid, elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid + 1, else: right = mid - 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access arr[2] = 3. Step 2: Compare 3 to target 3: equal. Step 3: No index update needed.
Step 1: Access arr[2] = 3. Step 2: Compare 3 to target 5: less. Step 3: Update index to 3 for next step.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Index-Based Access
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as ordered collections with zero-based indexing.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays, which are fundamental data structures in programming. An array is an ordered collection of elements, each accessible by an index starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays allow fast O(1) access to any element if you know its index, making them ideal for problems like binary search where we need to compare middle elements. For example, in an array arr = [10, 20, 30], arr[0] is 10, arr[1] is 20, and arr[2] is 30. The length of the array is the number of elements, so valid indices range from 0 to length-1. Accessing an index outside this range causes an error. This concept is prerequisite because the hybrid approach relies on accessing array elements at specific indices during binary search and linear expansion. Time complexity for access is O(1), space depends on the number of elements. Connection to L4: This enables the element comparisons. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in math but not programming), or forgetting that arrays are zero-indexed. Real code pattern: Declare an array like arr = [1, 2, 3], access with arr[i] where i is an integer index.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has elements at indices: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15, 3->20. Step 2: Access arr[2]: returns 15.
Step 1: Array has elements at indices: 0->100, 1->200. Step 2: Access arr[0]: returns 100.
